steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: Left of the blank is one independent clause: "Lucia Macchia...studies socioeconomic inequality." Right of the blank is another independent clause: "other researchers investigate areas such as organizational behavior and personal finance." In this sentence, "for instance" belongs as a parenthetical after Macchia's example, and the semicolon should separate the two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘inequality, for instance;’: "...studies socioeconomic inequality, for instance; other researchers investigate..." Comma before "for instance" sets it off as a parenthetical; semicolon after separates the two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘inequality; for instance,’: “...studies socioeconomic inequality; for instance, other researchers investigate organizational behavior and personal finance.” Here the semicolon falls before “for instance,” making the entire second clause (“other researchers investigate organizational behavior and personal finance”) an instance of Macchia studying inequality — which is illogical; other researchers’ different fields cannot be examples of Macchia’s own work. The correct response places the semicolon after “for instance,” so “for instance” is a parenthetical marking Macchia as one example of a researcher, and the semicolon then introduces a parallel statement about other researchers in different fields — logically and grammatically sound. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘inequality, for instance,’: "...studies socioeconomic inequality, for instance, other researchers investigate..." Two commas create a comma splice — the two independent clauses are joined only by commas without a semicolon. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘inequality for instance;’: "...studies socioeconomic inequality for instance; other researchers..." No comma before "for instance" leaves it unset as a parenthetical, creating a run-on up to the semicolon. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
